summary:
The Rwandan Genocide of 1994 was the systematic mass killing of the Tutsi ethnic minority and moderate Hutu by Hutu extremist militias and government forces over approximately 100 days from April to July 1994, resulting in the deaths of an estimated 500,000 to 800,000 people — roughly 70 percent of the Tutsi population and 20 percent of Rwanda's total population. It was one of the fastest genocides in recorded history, as the rate of killing exceeded even that of the Holocaust during its most intensive phases.

Rwanda's ethnic tensions had been deliberately sharpened by Belgian colonial rule, which introduced identity cards distinguishing Hutu and Tutsi and favored the Tutsi minority as colonial administrators. Post-independence violence in the 1960s and 1970s drove hundreds of thousands of Tutsi into exile, where many eventually joined the [[event:3665|Rwandan Patriotic Front]] (RPF). The 1990 RPF invasion from Uganda ignited the [[event:3665|Rwandan Civil War]], which ended with the Arusha Accords in August 1993 — a peace agreement that Hutu extremists within the government were determined to sabotage.

The genocide was triggered on April 6, 1994, when the plane carrying Rwandan President Juvénal Habyarimana was shot down. Hutu extremists — who may have arranged the assassination themselves — used it as a pretext to begin organized killing. Roadblocks were set up across the country within hours. Radio stations broadcast lists of Tutsi to be killed and provided killing instructions. Interahamwe militias, trained and armed by the government, conducted systematic massacres. Neighbors killed neighbors. Churches where Tutsi sought refuge became killing fields.

The international community's response was catastrophic in its inadequacy. The UN peacekeeping force present in Rwanda, under Canadian General Roméo Dallaire, was forbidden by the UN Security Council to intervene militarily despite Dallaire's advance warnings of the planned genocide. The United States and other Western nations deliberately avoided using the word "genocide," which would have obligated action under the Genocide Convention. France, which had close ties to the Hutu government, provided cover for fleeing genocidaires. The failure of international intervention in Rwanda became one of the defining failures of the post-Cold War world order.

The genocide ended when the RPF military forces conquered Rwanda in July 1994, driving the genocidal government and approximately two million Hutu refugees — fearing reprisals — into neighboring Zaire. This refugee crisis destabilized the entire Great Lakes region of Africa, contributing to the [[event:3667|First]] and Second Congo Wars. The [[event:3477|aftermath]] of the genocide required massive international humanitarian, judicial, and reconstruction efforts. The International Criminal Tribunal for Rwanda prosecuted senior perpetrators, while Rwanda's own gacaca community courts processed hundreds of thousands of lower-level cases.

Rwanda's recovery under President Paul Kagame since the genocide has been economically impressive, with sustained growth and development indicators, though the political system has remained authoritarian. The genocide's memory is central to Rwandan national identity and has been invoked repeatedly in debates about humanitarian intervention, international responsibility to protect civilian populations, and the capabilities of the United Nations system to prevent mass atrocities.
